title: Ionic bonding — how metals and non-metals stick together
examTip: Ionic bonding questions appear on every AQA Chemistry paper. Know how to draw dot-and-cross diagrams for NaCl, MgO and CaCl₂, and be able to explain conductivity using the idea of free ions.
introTips: This lesson covers how metals and non-metals bond by transferring electrons — and why that transfer creates the strong forces that hold ionic compounds together.
You'll learn to draw dot-and-cross diagrams, which AQA asks for regularly, and why they're the clearest way to show what's happening at atom level.
You'll also learn why ionic compounds have such high melting points and why they only conduct electricity when molten or dissolved — both classic exam questions.
intro: Table salt. The stuff on your chips. It's made of sodium — a metal that explodes in water — and chlorine — a toxic gas. Yet together they're completely safe, stable, and sit in a shaker on every kitchen table. How? Electrons. When a metal meets a non-metal, electrons move from one atom to the other, creating opposite charges that pull together hard enough to form a rigid, giant lattice. Understanding ionic bonding explains not just salt, but a huge range of materials — and it's a guaranteed topic on your AQA paper.

keyPoints.heading: Electron transfer — how ions form
keyPoints.content: Ionic bonding happens when a metal atom transfers one or more electrons to a non-metal atom. The metal loses electrons and becomes a positively charged cation. The non-metal gains electrons and becomes a negatively charged anion. The opposite charges attract each other — that's the ionic bond. Both atoms end up with a full outer shell, which is the stable, low-energy arrangement they're 'trying' to reach.
writeBullets: Metal loses electrons → becomes positively charged cation
Non-metal gains electrons → becomes negatively charged anion
Opposite charges attract — this electrostatic attraction is the ionic bond
Both ions end up with a full outer electron shell

keyTerms.def: The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer
A positively charged ion — formed when an atom loses electrons
A negatively charged ion — formed when an atom gains electrons

keyPoints.memTip: Cats are positive, ANions are negative — CATion = pOSITIVE. Or remember: 'Metals LOSE, Non-metals GAIN' → LEO the lion says GER (Lose Electrons Oxidation, Gain Electrons Reduction). Draw a simple arrow from Na to Cl with '1e⁻' above it to cement the picture.

keyPoints.heading: Predicting ion charges from the periodic table
keyPoints.content: You don't need to memorise every ion charge — the group number tells you. Group 1 metals form +1 ions (e.g. Na⁺, K⁺). Group 2 metals form +2 ions (e.g. Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺). Group 3 metals form +3 ions (e.g. Al³⁺). Group 6 non-metals form −2 ions (e.g. O²⁻). Group 7 non-metals form −1 ions (e.g. Cl⁻, F⁻). AQA also expects you to know these polyatomic ions: OH⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₃²⁻.

keyTerms.def: The column in the periodic table — determines how many electrons a main-group element loses or gains to form an ion

keyPoints.memTip: Think of a 'charge mirror' in the periodic table: Group 1 → +1, Group 2 → +2, Group 3 → +3. Then flip to the right side: Group 7 → −1, Group 6 → −2. Write the six common polyatomic ions on a sticky note (OH⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₃²⁻) and stick it somewhere you look every day until they're automatic.

keyPoints.heading: Dot-and-cross diagrams for ionic compounds
keyPoints.content: A dot-and-cross diagram shows electron transfer visually. Draw the outer shell electrons of each atom, then show electrons moving from the metal to the non-metal. The resulting ions are drawn with their new electron configurations in square brackets, with the charge written outside. AQA regularly asks for NaCl (1 transfer), MgO (2 transfer) and CaCl₂ (1 transfer to each Cl — so two Cl atoms needed). You only need to show outer shell electrons.
writeBullets: Draw outer shell electrons as dots (one atom) and crosses (the other)
Show arrow(s) for electron transfer from metal to non-metal
Put each ion in square brackets with the charge outside
CaCl₂: Ca gives 1e⁻ to each of 2 Cl atoms → [Ca]²⁺ 2[Cl]⁻

keyTerms.def: A diagram showing the outer shell electrons of atoms and ions, using dots for one species and crosses for the other

keyPoints.memTip: Practice drawing all three (NaCl, MgO, CaCl₂) from scratch without looking. Time yourself — aim to do any one in under 90 seconds in an exam. The square brackets and the charge outside are the two details students most often forget.

keyPoints.heading: Giant ionic lattice — structure and properties
keyPoints.content: Ionic compounds don't just form pairs of ions — they form a giant ionic lattice: a regular 3D arrangement of millions of positive and negative ions, each surrounded by ions of the opposite charge. The electrostatic forces between them act in all directions and are very strong. This gives ionic compounds high melting and boiling points — it takes a lot of energy to break up the lattice. In the solid state, ions are fixed in position so ionic compounds cannot conduct electricity. When molten or dissolved in water, the ions are free to move and carry charge — so they conduct.
writeBullets: Giant ionic lattice = regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions
Strong electrostatic forces in all directions → very high melting and boiling points
Solid: ions fixed in place → does NOT conduct electricity
Molten or dissolved: ions free to move → DOES conduct electricity

keyTerms.def: A regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions held together by strong electrostatic forces
The attraction between opposite charges — the force that holds an ionic lattice together

keyPoints.memTip: Remember 'FREE TO MOVE, FREE TO CONDUCT' — ions only conduct when they can move. Solid = fixed = no conduct. Molten or dissolved = free = conducts. Draw a quick diagram of a lattice and cross out the conductivity symbol, then redraw it melting and add a tick. That visual contrast helps it stick.

tables.title: Common ions to know for AQA

3.question: A solid ionic compound is connected to a circuit. What happens and why?
options: It conducts electricity because it contains charged particles
It does not conduct electricity because the ions are fixed in the lattice
It does not conduct electricity because it has no charged particles
It conducts electricity because electrons move through the lattice
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right — the ions ARE there, but they're fixed in the lattice and cannot move, so no charge can flow.
3.wrongFeedback: Ionic compounds do contain charged particles (ions), but in the solid state those ions are locked in the lattice and cannot move. Conductivity requires free-moving charged particles — and solid ions are not free.

commonMistakes: Ionic compounds contain ions, not molecules — never describe NaCl as a 'molecule'. It's a lattice of ions.
Ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved because the ions become free to move — in the solid state the ions are there but fixed, so conduction is impossible.
When drawing dot-and-cross diagrams, put each ion inside square brackets and write the charge outside the bracket — both are required for the mark.
Group 7 elements form −1 ions (one electron gained to fill the outer shell), not −7 ions. The charge comes from the number of electrons needed, not the group number directly.
examTips: Whenever you describe why an ionic compound conducts electricity, you must say: (1) ions are present AND (2) they are free to move. Both points are needed.
For dot-and-cross questions: show only outer shell electrons, use square brackets, and write the correct charge outside — these three details earn or lose marks.
AQA may describe a substance with a high melting point — mention the giant ionic lattice and strong electrostatic forces in all directions.
revisionCardBullets: Ionic bonding = transfer of electrons from metal to non-metal
Metal loses electrons → cation (positive) | Non-metal gains electrons → anion (negative)
Polyatomic ions: OH⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₃²⁻
Dot-and-cross: outer shells only, square brackets, charge outside
NaCl: 1 transfer | MgO: 2 transfer | CaCl₂: Ca gives 1e⁻ to each of 2 Cl
Giant ionic lattice = regular 3D arrangement of ions held by strong electrostatic forces
High melting/boiling points — lots of energy needed to break the lattice
Solid = ions fixed = does NOT conduct | Molten or dissolved = ions free = DOES conduct
